name_en: Rabbi Yisrael ben Eliezer, the Baal Shem Tov
bio: Founder of the chassidic movement. Son of Rabbi Eliezer, known for his extraordinary love of every Jew and his teaching that every person can serve G-d through joy and simple faith. His teachings became the spiritual foundation that the Alter Rebbe later developed into Chabad chassidus.

common_name: Rabbi Eliezer (father of Baal Shem Tov)
bio: Father of the Baal Shem Tov. Renowned for his extraordinary dedication to hachnasat orchim (hosting guests), using the vast majority of his wealth to host 20-30 poor and lonely guests every Shabbat. Before his passing, when his son Yisrael was about five years old, he left him two foundational commands: to fear no one but G-d, and to love every single Jew unconditionally. These became core principles of the chassidic movement his son would later establish.

name_en: Rabbi Tzvi, son of the Baal Shem Tov
name_he: רבי צבי בן הבעל שם טוב
common_name: Rabbi Tzvi (son of the Baal Shem Tov)
bio: Son of the Baal Shem Tov, primarily remembered for an extraordinary display of humility and self-nullification regarding the leadership of the chassidic movement.

name_en: Rabbi Dovber, the Maggid of Mezeritch
bio: Primary successor to the Baal Shem Tov and the teacher who systematized chassidic teaching. The Alter Rebbe was among his greatest disciples, and the journey of the Alter Rebbe to Mezeritch became foundational lore in Chabad.

A gaon, tzaddik, and devoted disciple of the Baal Shem Tov, who served as a special emissary of his master. He undertook a pivotal mission to Vilna to the Gaon Rabbi Shlomo Yochanan, conveying the Baal Shem Tov's message that his son Chaim Eliyahu should travel to study in Vitebsk.

common_name: Rabbi Baruch (father of the Alter Rebbe)
bio: Father of the Alter Rebbe. Known for his profound humility, self-imposed exile, and prophetic foresight regarding his son's greatness. Owned a farm where the hidden tzaddik R' Chaim Eliyahu would stay for weeks at a time, becoming a historical witness to the young Alter Rebbe's wondrous talents.

journey.event: Visited the Baal Shem Tov and received a blessing for a son

bio: An outstanding disciple of the Baal Shem Tov, despite being the son of Rabbi Shlomo Yochanan, one of the great Torah geniuses of Vilna. After his father was visited by R' Shimon Zundel of Trisk on a mission from the Baal Shem Tov, he traveled to Vitebsk to study among the hidden tzaddikim there. Became a close friend of R' Baruch, the father of the Alter Rebbe, and a key witness to the Alter Rebbe's childhood greatness.

A prominent and highly respected member of his community who earned the title 'Baal Shem' (Master of the Name) because he engaged in providing spiritual remedies and healing incantations. Featured in a frightening, prophetic dream experienced by the Alter Rebbe at age ten in the synagogue of Liozna.

Founder of Chabad chassidus. Author of Tanya and the Shulchan Aruch HaRav. Disciple of the Maggid of Mezeritch. Established Chabad as a distinct intellectual stream of chassidus. Imprisoned by the Czarist government in 1798 and 1800; his release on Yud-Tes Kislev is celebrated as the 'Rosh Hashanah of Chassidus.' Died in 1812 while fleeing Napoleon's advance.

A chassid of the Alter Rebbe and older brother of Rabbi Zev Vilenker (his senior by twenty years). Was assigned by the Alter Rebbe, together with R' Moshe Meizlish, to a covert mission to edit and compile intelligence on the antisemitic writings of the Russian statesman Derzhavin, ultimately securing the safety of the Jewish community in Russia by influencing the Tsar to burn the hateful report.

Younger brother of R' Moshe Vilenker by twenty years; a chassid of the Alter Rebbe. Drawn to chassidus in the poor districts of Vilna by observing chassidim celebrating joyously over the persecution of a colleague. Famous for telling his older brother who asked for stories of miracles: 'I am giving you the marrow of the oil, and you are asking me for the toenails.' His home in Vitebsk continued to be a center for chassidic gatherings for generations.

One of the foremost intellectual thinkers among the students of the Alter Rebbe. A tremendous scholar in revealed Torah and chassidic philosophy. Earned his livelihood as a lumber merchant, famously found standing motionless in the middle of a busy lumber fair, deep in meditation on the greatness of the Creator. The Alter Rebbe declared that when he prayed 'Sim Shalom,' the Yechidah of his soul shone within him, higher than the Revelation of Elijah.

journey.event: Lived as a merchant and devoted Chassid

bio: A prominent and highly respected chassid of the Alter Rebbe and the Mitteler Rebbe, who served as the Rabbi of Krāslava. Famous for his fiery passion during the Torah reading of Parshas Zachor—a sight that drew R' Binyamin Kletzker to the Alter Rebbe's movement. Traveled extensively to spread chassidus, sometimes ascending the synagogue bimah disguised as a traveling maggid.

A devoted chassid of the Alter Rebbe credited with sparking a historic spiritual revolution in the town of Dribin, successfully transforming its leading, staunchly opposed Torah scholars—including Rabbi Zerach Meir and his students—into devoted chassidim of the Alter Rebbe.

A tremendous Torah scholar (Talmid Chacham Atzum) who lived in Dribin during the era of the Alter Rebbe. A sixty-year-old sage who never ceased his Torah study for even a moment. Through the influence of R' Menachem Zecharia, he and his students transformed into devoted chassidim of the Alter Rebbe.

One of the greatest and most prominent chassidim of the Alter Rebbe and the Mitteler Rebbe, both of whom held him in great affection. A recognized Torah genius and serious intellectual who served as a chozer—responsible for memorizing, reviewing, and repeating the Alter Rebbe's chassidic discourses. Witness to the Alter Rebbe's extraordinary state of deveikus during the Torah reading of Bereishis.

journey.event: Lived as a devoted disciple and scholar

bio: A prominent and wealthy chassid of the Alter Rebbe and the Mitteler Rebbe, regarded as a true 'Baal Madrega' and considered to have achieved the spiritual rank of a Beinoni as defined in Tanya. Used his connections within the Russian royal court to assist the Rebbes and the Jewish community. Played a crucial role in locating the Alter Rebbe during his 1798 imprisonment in the St. Petersburg fortress by hiding a note in a plate of fruit preserve. The Alter Rebbe stayed at his spacious Petersburg home after his release.

A renowned chassid known for his extraordinary devotion, intense prayer, and total immersion in chassidic philosophy. Lived in Lepli, Belarus, earning a modest living as a salt merchant. Famously refused the Alter Rebbe's offer of a blessing for wealth, fearing it would distract him from divine service; accepted a blessing for longevity only on condition his life would be spiritually meaningful. Continued as a devoted follower of the Tzemach Tzedek.

A prominent historical figure who lived in Shvintzian (also associated with Vilna). A highly respected gaon, wealthy merchant, and initially one of the fiercest opponents of the chassidic movement before becoming connected to the Alter Rebbe.

name_en: Rabbi Dovber Schneuri, the Mitteler Rebbe
bio: Second Lubavitcher Rebbe. Son and successor of the Alter Rebbe. Established his court in Lubavitch in 1813. His chassidic discourses are considered 'pure chassidus'—the most expansive and detailed chassidic philosophy. Sent Reb Hillel Paritcher to spread chassidus through towns and villages. Arrested and released on Yud Kislev 1826, an event celebrated annually.

A towering Chabad chassid, profound Torah scholar, and exemplary 'Oved' (one whose divine service focuses on emotional refinement and prayer). Served as Rabbi of Paritch and later Bobruisk. Originally a follower of Rabbi Mordechai of Chernobyl before joining the Mitteler Rebbe's court. The Tzemach Tzedek categorized him as an 'Oved' in contrast to R' Yitzchak Isaac of Homel ('Maskil'). Famously said that after studying Tanya, he realized he was not even a Beinoni. Passed away in Kherson in 1864.

name_en: Rabbi Yitzchak Isaac Epstein of Homel
bio: A towering intellectual and one of the most prominent chassidim of the Alter Rebbe, the Mitteler Rebbe, and the Tzemach Tzedek. The Tzemach Tzedek categorized him as a 'Maskil' (intellectual), in contrast to his peer R' Hillel of Paritch ('Oved'). Served as Rabbi of Vitebsk. In his youth served as a badchan at weddings, dedicating earnings to support Jews in the Holy Land. Sent by the Tzemach Tzedek as emissary to Rabbi Yisrael of Ruzhin.

name_en: Rabbi Yitzchak Isaac of Vitebsk (era of the Tzemach Tzedek)
bio: An elder chassidic master prominent during the leadership of the Tzemach Tzedek. Traveled to Lubavitch for Shavuot 1857, among a massive gathering of over 120 rabbis and prominent elder chassidim, including R' Yitzchak Isaac of Homel and R' Hillel of Paritch.

journey.event: Served as a prominent Chassid and scholar

name_en: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneersohn, the Tzemach Tzedek
bio: Third Lubavitcher Rebbe. Grandson of the Alter Rebbe; son of Rabbi Shalom Shachna. Author of the responsa Tzemach Tzedek and numerous chassidic works. His sons established multiple Chabad dynasties including Lubavitch, Kapust, Niezhin, and Avritch. Established the surname 'Schneersohn' for his descendants.

Eldest son of the Tzemach Tzedek. A prominent and devoted figure who served his father faithfully until passing after a short illness, predeceasing his father. Father of Rabbi Zalman who was assigned 'weekly mitzvot' by the Tzemach Tzedek and was noted for his self-sacrifice.

Son of the Tzemach Tzedek. Established the Kapust branch of Chabad. Father of Rabbi Chaim Shlomo Zalman Yosef Schneersohn. The family surname 'Schneersohn' officially began with the Tzemach Tzedek's sons; the Maharil originally used the surname 'Schneuri'.

name_en: Rabbi Chaim Shlomo Zalman Yosef Schneersohn
name_he: רבי חיים שלמה זלמן יוסף שניאורסאהן
bio: Son of the Maharil of Kapust (Rabbi Yehuda Leib), grandson of the Tzemach Tzedek. Recognized from a young age for extraordinary spiritual level and intellectual depth. His grandfather, the Tzemach Tzedek, would assign him 'weekly mitzvot' along with his cousins.

A chassid recorded as having described his very first visit to the Tzemach Tzedek. Recalled the majestic moments of awe and glory he experienced when entering his first yechidus with the Rebbe alongside his father.

name_en: Rabbi Shneur Zalman Fradkin (Gaon of Lublin)
bio: Renowned as the Gaon of Lublin and the author of the halachic work Torat Chesed. A vast genius famous for his extraordinary memory—he could see a book briefly and know its contents by heart. A devoted chassid of the Tzemach Tzedek. The Tzemach Tzedek once asked him a Mishnah he could not remember, explaining the Gaon was in such a state of bittul that his ego had vanished. In his later years moved to Jerusalem and merited to teach the future Rebbe Rayatz.

Fourth Lubavitcher Rebbe. Youngest son of the Tzemach Tzedek and successor in Lubavitch. Famous for the motto 'L'chatchila Ariber'—leaping over obstacles from the start. Honored with writing the final words of a secret Torah scroll completed in Lubavitch in 1881.

A revered chassid and elder mashpia known for his profound insight and his extreme care in choosing his words. Worked in the timber trade. A meticulous transmitter of chassidic history, preserving accounts of the secret 1881 Torah scroll completion in Lubavitch and the Tzemach Tzedek's altering of the order of yechidus. Whispered at the funeral of R' Shilem Kuratin: 'For sixteen years he served the Holy One, blessed be He.'

name_en: Rabbi Sholom DovBer Schneersohn, the Rashab
bio: Fifth Lubavitcher Rebbe. Founded the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva network in 1897, considered one of his greatest legacies. Fled Lubavitch in 1915 during WWI to Rostov-on-Don. His chassidic discourses, especially the Hemshech Tarsav (1906) and Toras Shalom, are foundational. Sometimes called 'the Maimonides of Chassidus' for his systematic treatment of chassidic philosophy.

Wife of the Rashab and mother of the Rayatz. Active in the Rebbe Rashab's court. Asked about specific family relationships and traditions, including why the Rashab referred to her father in letters as 'my son-in-law' and another relative as 'my grandson.' Was present when chassidim came for yechidus and noted that whenever the Rashab received people, 'the floor is left wet with tears.'

A highly revered mashpia in the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in Lubavitch. Renowned for absolute, uncompromising inner authenticity (pnimiyus), intense focus on the service of prayer, and profound dedication to G-d. The Rashab said of him after his passing: 'There is no one like Shilem,' and rejected proposed successors as not being 'pnimiyim like Shilem.' R' Alter Shimchovitz testified that he served G-d to the absolute maximum capacity that a created being could possibly reach.

name_en: Rabbi Yitzchak Horowitz, Reb Itche Masmid
name_he: רבי יצחק האראוויץ - ר' איטשע מתמיד
bio: A legendary chassid of the Rashab and the Rayatz, universally known as 'the diligent one' for his unparalleled diligence in Torah study. Served as a wandering emissary (Shadar) for the Rebbe Rayatz across Russia, Europe, and the United States to raise funds for Chabad institutions. Lived a life almost entirely detached from worldly matters—fasted frequently, ate one casual meal a day, barely slept. Murdered Al Kiddush Hashem in Riga in 1941/1942.

A highly revered and beloved chassid of the Rashab, the Rayatz, and the Lubavitcher Rebbe. Renowned for profound inner emotional service (pnimiyus), exactness in transmitting chassidic traditions, and fiery dedication to truth. Lost his wife and sons during the Communist Revolution and arrived in the Holy Land lonely and destitute. The Rashab called him an 'angel' and said his niggun 'comes from the forest.' Traveled to the Rebbe's court in New York in Adar 1947 and remained there until his passing on Erev Shavuos 1949.

Chief Rabbi of Bnei Brak in Israel. In his younger years served as the 'Rav of the Court' (Rav HaChatzer) for the Rashab, gaining unparalleled firsthand knowledge of the Rebbe's halachic rulings and personal customs. The Lubavitcher Rebbe frequently consulted him on the exact practices of the Rashab, including specifications for constructing a mikvah. Witnessed the only known photograph of the Rashab being taken in Rostov 1919/1920.

A legendary mashpia and scholar who served as a primary model for chassidic avoda. In 1912 selected by the Rashab among an elite group sent to Hebron to establish Yeshiva Torat Emet; later a central figure at the Jerusalem branch. Served as mashpia of the Chabad yeshiva in Warsaw during the interwar period; one of his prominent students was Rabbi Moshe Ashkenazi. A chozer of the Rashab's discourses. Refused a prestigious burial plot, insisting on being buried 'in the ground like everyone else.'

A towering chassidic figure known for profound emotional service, sharp and uncompromising truthfulness, and absolute devotion to the Rebbes of Chabad. Lived in Russia (including Nevel) and later in Tel Aviv and Kfar Chabad. Coined the phrase, 'Whoever does not have the current Rebbe, did not have the previous Rebbe either.' Major chassidic mentors of the next generation including R' Mendel Futerfas and R' Nissan Nemenov attributed their chassidic vitality directly to his influence. Passed away on 3 Shvat 1952.

name_en: Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn, the Rayatz
name_he: רבי יוסף יצחק שניאורסאהן - אדמו"ר הריי"צ
bio: Sixth Lubavitcher Rebbe. Son and successor of the Rashab. Led Chabad through the Soviet persecution; arrested by the OGPU in 1927 and sentenced to death, his release on Yud-Beis Tammuz is celebrated annually. Escaped through Riga, Warsaw, and Otwock; arrived in the United States in 1940 and established the Lubavitch headquarters at 770 Eastern Parkway in Brooklyn. Built underground networks across Soviet Russia through emissaries like R' Chonya Morozov, R' Simcha Gorodetsky, and R' Itche Masmid.

Father of the Lubavitcher Rebbe. A renowned Torah scholar, master of Kabbalah, and Chief Rabbi of Yekaterinoslav (Dnepropetrovsk) in Ukraine. His rabbinic ordination was granted by Rabbi Chaim of Brisk. The Rashab listed him among three specific chassidim about whom he said, 'When one of these three asks something, you have to know what to answer.' Courageously declared at a Soviet rabbinical conference that he would not sign a false document even if it meant giving up his life. Ultimately gave his life in actual mesiras nefesh under Soviet persecution.

Mother of the Lubavitcher Rebbe. Wife of Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson. Stood by her husband through exile and persecution under the Soviet regime. After his passing, she eventually was brought to Brooklyn where she lived near her son.

journey.event: Moved with her husband for his Rabbinic post

bio: Brother of the Lubavitcher Rebbe; son of Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson. R' Simcha Gorodetsky witnessed him and his father completing an entire Talmudic tractate every single night during a visit to Yekaterinoslav. Later in life he frequently farbrenged with R' Nachum Goldschmid in Tel Aviv.

Personal secretary to the Rebbe Rayatz and manager of the entire underground Jewish network throughout the Soviet Union. A brilliant maskil in chassidus and a highly elevated servant of G-d. The Rashab gifted him with profound love and fear of G-d. On Tisha B'Av he would weep over the destruction of the Temple as if it were happening then; on Purim distributed Mishloach Manos all day with boundless joy. Martyred for his holy work under the Soviets (Hy"d).

A towering Chabad figure who served as Rosh Yeshiva and mashpia of the Chabad yeshivas in Tel Aviv and Rishon LeZion, where he also served as rabbi of the chassidic community. Renowned for profound Torah knowledge, sharpness, pedagogical mastery, and fierce dedication to authentic chassidic life. A smile never left his face, even during the most difficult times. Often told his students: 'We are all chassidim of the Rebbe, and I am merely the match that lights the fire.'

Legendary mashpia of the Tomchei Temimim yeshivas in Russia, Tel Aviv, Lod, and ultimately Kfar Chabad. The Rayatz appointed him mashpia in Rostov at age 25, telling him, 'This is a time of war. We take soldiers and make them officers.' The worst label he could give a person was 'Chitzon' (superficial). The Rebbe attested to his unmatched impact: 'The only place where one receives true chassidic grounding is by R' Shlomo Chaim.' Passed away 19 Iyar 5731 (1971).

A foundational figure in establishing the Chabad-Lubavitch community and its institutions in the Holy Land. Subdued his natural inclination toward private Torah study to fulfill the Rebbe's directives in public activism. Established a yeshiva and cheder in Tel Aviv per the Rayatz's directives. Succeeded R' Shlomo Yosef Zevin as rabbi of the Chabad synagogue on Montefiore Street. His Tel Aviv home (corner of Rothschild and Mazeh) was the nerve center of Chabad activities in Israel, called by R' Zusha Wilimovsky 'an institution of the Rebbe.'

journey.event: Immigrated to the Holy Land and served as Rabbi

bio: A legendary mashpia and living symbol of mesiras nefesh. Chief organizer of the 1946 underground 'Exodus from Egypt' that smuggled hundreds of chassidic families out of the Soviet Union via Lviv. Captured and sentenced to eight years in a Siberian labor camp. Maintained strict kashrus and spiritual integrity throughout. Later served as primary mashpia in Kfar Chabad, shaping a generation of students through profound, raw, authentic chassidic teachings.

A towering chassidic figure, profound thinker, and devoted soldier of Chabad. Served as a primary mashpia in the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva and as personal secretary to the Rebbe Rayatz. Led the underground yeshiva network in Soviet Russia. Taught that the entirety of chassidic philosophy boiled down to one point: 'The world is not a reality at all.' Hy"d.

A central pillar of the Chabad community in Tel Aviv, a devoted emissary of the Rayatz in the Holy Land. Categorized by the Rebbe as one of the key figures who took part in the Rayatz's historic liberation. Passed away on 9 Iyar 1963 and was buried in Tzfat.

A prominent mashpia, central pillar of the Chabad community in Tel Aviv, and legendary expositor of chassidic philosophy. The Rayatz remarked that establishing the entire Tomchei Temimim yeshiva network would have been worthwhile even if only to produce Reb Nachum. Known as 'the explainer of chassidus to the masses'—his Tanya classes were broadcast on radio. Israel's President Zalman Shazar and author S.Y. Agnon attended his farbrengens. Dubbed 'Peh Mefik Margaliyot' (a mouth producing pearls) by Reb Itche Masmid.

A prominent chassid, profound intellectual in chassidus, and dedicated communal activist in the Holy Land. The Rayatz held him in extremely high regard, saying activists in the Holy Land must be 'ours—like Leizer [Karasik] and Meishke.' His home on Feierberg Street in Tel Aviv was the ultimate destination for anyone seeking to clarify difficult concepts in chassidus.

A prominent Chabad rabbi and author who immigrated to the Holy Land. Served as rabbi of the Neve Tzedek neighborhood in Jaffa, succeeding the veteran Chabad rabbi Rabbi Shneur Zalman Slonim. Remembered for deep wisdom, level-headed judgment, and profound dedication to teaching chassidic philosophy.

A prominent and deeply emotional chassid renowned for phenomenal memory, intense spiritual passion, and a foundational role in preserving and transmitting chassidic history. Father of R' Yoel Kahn, the Rebbe's chief chozer. Immigrated to the Holy Land in the 1930s, residing in Ramat Gan, Tel Aviv, and eventually Kfar Chabad. Suffered three years of exile in Siberia for studying Torah and spreading Judaism, maintaining a deep correspondence with R' Yankel Zurovitzer during that time.

A legendary and fiery chassid celebrated for his profound, uncompromising hiskashrus to the Rebbes of Chabad. As a Tomchei Temimim student around 1898 in Lubavitch, he secretly copied the Rashab's unreleased marginal notes on Torah Or—when discovered, the Rashab praised him saying, 'A blessing upon him, and I wish others would steal too!' Played a central role corresponding with the seventh Rebbe to plead with him to accept the leadership in 1951.

A highly revered and authentic chassid who embodied the core educational values of Tomchei Temimim—serving G-d and showing immense love for his fellow Jews in every situation, always out of joy and a good heart. Maintained close correspondence with R' Folleh Kahn during the latter's Siberian exile, sending him deep chassidic insights regarding prayer and the holidays. Martyred Al Kiddush Hashem under the Soviets; the Rebbe established his yahrzeit as the 24th of Teves.

A legendary chassid, devoted Shadar of the Rayatz, and towering symbol of mesiras nefesh who survived decades of communist persecution. In 1923 sent by the Rayatz to establish a network of underground Torah schools in the Bukhara region, gathering over 800 students under 45 teachers in Samarkand within three months. Endured 10 years of imprisonment and exile. Finally permitted to leave Russia in 1964 and moved to Israel, establishing a Talmud Torah for Bukharan immigrants in Kfar Chabad.

A scholarly and deeply devoted chassid whose life bridged Soviet Russia and the rebuilding of Chabad in Bnei Brak, Israel. Disabled by a leg wound from WWII military service, he used his condition as cover to teach Jewish children in an underground cheder in Tashkent (organized by R' Aharon Chazan). Known for immense Torah genius, fiery chassidic spirit, sharp wit, and self-sacrifice. After immigrating, became a primary speaker and spiritual pillar of the Chabad synagogue in Bnei Brak.

A prominent mashpia known for sharp intellect, profound connection to the Rebbe, and absolute precision in transmitting chassidic history. Initially served as mashpia in the Chabad Yeshiva in Kiryat Gat. In 1972 the Rebbe instructed him to establish a Yeshiva for Russian immigrants in Nachlat Har Chabad (Kiryat Malachi). A student of R' Mendel Futerfas. Provided profound chassidic guidance to the community following 3 Tammuz 1994.

A towering, legendary chassid considered by his peers from the original Lubavitch yeshiva to have reached the rare spiritual level of a Beinoni. Immigrated to the Holy Land in 1935, living in Jerusalem, Ramat Gan, and ultimately Bnei Brak. Despite his brilliant mind, chose to work as a simple construction worker so his mind would be free for Torah and chassidus. The Rebbe approved the inscription on his tombstone: 'He was careful in thought, speech, and action all the days of his life.'

A profound mashpia and influential figure who led a small but vibrant Chabad community in Ramat Gan. Known for immense success in translating deep chassidic concepts into accessible, modern language. Transmitted teachings from his Warsaw mashpia, Rabbi Alter Simchovitz, regarding the inner perfection of R' Shilem Kuratin.

A towering chassidic figure, devoted emissary, and central pillar of the Chabad community in Jerusalem. Born in the Holy Land, traveled to Lubavitch in his youth where he studied in Tomchei Temimim and received rare close attention from the Rebbes. A disciple of Reb Leizer Chechersker. During his youth regularly ate at the home of Rebbetzin Rivkah and on Shabbos and holidays at the table of the Rashab.

A legendary mashpia and principal of the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in Brunoy, France. Universally revered for unparalleled, uncompromising kabbalas ol (acceptance of the Heavenly yoke) and intense daily practice of iskafya (subduing physical desires). His chassidic vitality was attributed to the profound influence of R' Zalman Moshe HaYitzchaki.

A deeply devoted chassid known for uncompromising dedication to Judaism and Jewish education under the oppressive Soviet regime. Managed an underground cheder in Russia; arrested in Elul 1937, imprisoned and tortured for over a year, miraculously released the day after Yom Kippur 1938. Upon learning his daughter had been sent to a Soviet school as a condition of his release, he declared he would return to prison immediately rather than subject her to communist secular education.

A central and highly influential figure in the early years of Kfar Chabad. Served as spiritual director (Menahel Ruchani) of the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in Lod, credited with successfully raising a chassidic generation among Sephardic youth. Married to the daughter of R' Zalman Moshe HaYitzchaki. Moved to New York in 1959. Renowned as both a profound maskil and a dedicated oved.

A towering figure in Chabad history who seamlessly combined strict rabbinic authority with pure, authentic chassidishkeit. Eulogized by Rabbi Yaakov Landau as the 'Peri Etz Hadar' of Lubavitch. Before moving to the Holy Land served as rabbi in several Soviet cities and as director and mashpia in the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in Shchedrin. Later served as rabbi of the Mea Shearim neighborhood in Jerusalem and as prominent Chabad rabbi and dayyan in Tel Aviv-Jaffa. Profoundly shaped the next generation of Chabad mentors including R' Chaim Shaul Brook and R' Shlomo Chaim Kesselman.

A prominent chassid and scholar who played a central role in establishing and nurturing the Chabad community in Jerusalem during the mid-20th century. Arrived in Israel in 1950 and served as rabbi for the Chabad community in Jerusalem, deepening chassidic life by encouraging study of chassidus, revitalizing the avoda of prayer, and connecting many to the Chabad Rebbes. After the passing of the Rayatz, boldly testified to the holiness of the seventh Rebbe: 'The Rebbe is a Rebbe!'

Son of Rabbi Nachum Shmaryahu Sasonkin; a legendary chassid remembered for fierce devotion, unyielding mesiras nefesh in Soviet Russia, and profound connection to the Lubavitcher Rebbe. Famously inspired teshuvah in a Jewish prisoner named Rozman by stepping forward and claiming to be him to spare him forced Shabbos work, then accepting solitary confinement under inhumane conditions. After miraculous release from Russia, moved to Jerusalem.

Served as rabbi of the Chabad community in Tel Aviv. Father of Rabbi Mordechai Shmuel Ashkenazi and son-in-law and successor of Rabbi Eliezer Karasik. Lived in his late father-in-law's home at the corner of Rothschild Boulevard and Maze Street in Tel Aviv. Educated by his great mashpia Rabbi Alter Shimchovitz. Acquired a special small Torah scroll that had a rich history in a Russian prison, which he brought as a gift to the Lubavitcher Rebbe, who used to dance with it during Hakafot of Simchat Torah.

A distinguished chassid and 'Tamim' at the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in Lubavitch during the era of the Rashab and the Rayatz. Rabbi Yaakov Landau counted him among the most wondrous and spiritually abstracted students, calling him an 'outstanding Tamim.' During a yechidus the Rashab told him, 'From your question, I see that you have elevated yourself more than I thought.'

Widely known as 'the Chozer'—the individual responsible for memorizing, reviewing, and transcribing the Rebbe's discourses. Son of R' Folleh Kahn. A preserver of the Rebbe's teachings and transmitter of remarkable stories about the Rebbe's personal care for individuals. Regularly sent transcripts (hanachos) of the Rebbe's discourses to his father in Israel. A trusted guide for other chassidim.

A prominent 'man of Halacha' and the author of the well-known book Sipurei Chassidim (Chassidic Tales). Served as rabbi of the Chabad synagogue in Tel Aviv before R' Eliezer Karasik succeeded him. Famously used a chassidic story to block a Tel Aviv municipal law banning beggars. In an article commemorating the 150th anniversary of the Alter Rebbe's passing, described him as 'a wondrous figure who generated entire eras and revolutionized Jewish worldview, lifestyle, and feeling.'

A prominent chassid best known for significant contributions to chassidic literature and his role in preserving the oral traditions of the movement. Authored two influential collections: Shmuot VeSippurim (Hearings and Stories) and Lubavitch VeChayaleha (Lubavitch and Its Soldiers). The Rebbe personally encouraged him to write and publish these stories, emphasizing that chassidic stories can sometimes awaken fear of Heaven more effectively than other teachings.

journey.event: Fled the outbreak of World War II

bio: A towering halachic authority and devoted chassid; author of Ketzot HaShulchan, Piskei HaSiddur, and Shiurei Tziyon. Established that an Amah is 48 centimeters and a Revi'it is 86.25 grams—standard halachic measurements in much of the Jewish world. Engaged in a famous dispute with the Chazon Ish over measurements, defending the Alter Rebbe's precision. Edited the Luach Kolel Chabad. Immediately after the Rayatz's passing, declared at a Jerusalem gathering: 'The golden chain will not break; the Ramash Shlita will continue it!'

Esteemed Mara D'asra (Chief Rabbi) of Kfar Chabad for twenty-five years, guiding the community with brilliance, firm authority, and absolute devotion to the Rebbe. Tragically killed on the night of 8 Sivan 1974 at age 94 when his vehicle overturned returning from Jerusalem to Kfar Chabad. The Rebbe dedicated an entire Sicha to this heartbreaking event. Succeeded as Mara D'asra by his son-in-law Rabbi Nachum Trebnik.

Arrived in the Holy Land in 1949 and was appointed Rabbi of the Chabad community in Lod and Rosh Yeshiva of the local Tomchei Temimim yeshiva. Despite severe financial hardship, rejected tempting offers for other rabbinic positions conditional upon joining 'Hapoel HaMizrachi.' Accepted the Lod position only after a direct written command from the Rayatz in Tishrei 5710.

A highly distinguished chassid and prominent student of the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva. The Rashab famously declared that the entire establishment of Tomchei Temimim was worthwhile just for the sake of producing two specific individuals: Rabbi David Horodoker and Rabbi Itche Masmid. A student of the elder chassid R' Michoel Bliner.

A prominent chassid who served as one of the great mashpiyim at the Toras Emes yeshiva in Jerusalem. Contributed to establishing and building the Chabad community in Jerusalem, alongside Rabbi David Goldberg and Rabbi Moshe Weber. Student and brother-in-law of R' Alter Shimchovitz; preserved and relayed many stories and teachings from his great mentor.

Served as a member of Agudas Chasidei Chabad in the United States. The designated figure responsible for handling complex and sensitive communal matters; in 1945 his expertise was required to deal with a severe problem in the Chicago Chabad community. A highly experienced fundraiser; Rabbi Shlomo Cunin accompanied him at the start of his shlichus specifically to learn the art of fundraising.

name_en: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson, the Lubavitcher Rebbe
bio: Seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe. Son of Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson and Rebbetzin Chana. Son-in-law of the Rayatz. Accepted the leadership of Chabad on 10 Shvat 1951. Built a global network of shluchim transforming Chabad into a worldwide movement. Edited and published the chassidic discourses and letters of the previous Rebbes. Suffered a heart attack in 1977 on Shemini Atzeret. Delivered the dramatic address of 28 Nissan 1991 calling on chassidim to 'do all you can to bring Moshiach.' Passed on 3 Tammuz 1994.

Wife of the Lubavitcher Rebbe and middle daughter of the Rayatz. Married the future Rebbe in Warsaw in 1928, an event the Rayatz officiated together with the leaders of the chassidic world. Together with her husband she fled Europe through Paris and Marseilles to New York. Known for her modesty, sharp intellect, and devotion to the Rebbe.

Arrived in the Holy Land in 1961 and became a central, inspiring figure in Kfar Chabad, where he worked as a melamed. Known for unceasing prayer and study, strict adherence to even the lightest customs, and ability to transmit the mesiras nefesh of Russian Jewry to younger generations. The Lubavitcher Rebbe instructed him to lay four pairs of Tefillin daily. Distinguished by his thick beard and Russian 'kasket' cap—the Rebbe instructed him not to replace it with a fedora as it reminded heaven of the self-sacrifice of Russian Jewry.
Rebbetzin Rivka was the mother of the Alter Rebbe and a deeply righteous woman. She traveled to the Baal Shem Tov in Mezhibuzh with her young son to have his hair cut and to receive a blessing.

journey.event: Traveled to the Baal Shem Tov for her son's haircut and blessing

bio: Rabbi Yehuda Leib was the brother of the Alter Rebbe and served as the rabbi of Yanovitch. He traveled with his brother to the Maggid of Mezritch and became a prominent Chassidic scholar. He authored the halachic work She'erit Yehuda.
Rabbi Mordechai was a brother of the Alter Rebbe. He was a recognized scholar who interacted with the Alter Rebbe's chassidim.

journey.event: Met the Alter Rebbe during a visit
Fled home to study in the Alter Rebbe's 'Cheder Aleph'
Relocated to the Vitebsk area to follow the Chassidic path

bio: Rebbetzin Devorah Leah was the daughter of the Alter Rebbe and mother of the Tzemach Tzedek. In 1792, she heroically swore before the Torah scrolls to sacrifice her own life in exchange for her father's continued life and leadership. She passed away shortly after and is buried in Liozna.

journey.event: Publicly vowed to exchange her life for her father's

bio: Rebbetzin Freida was a deeply Chassidic daughter of the Alter Rebbe. Her father held her in high esteem and would deliver profound Chassidic discourses directly to her, which her brother the Mitteler Rebbe would secretly listen to. The Tzemach Tzedek described her spiritual gifts as a direct revelation from above.

Rabbi Chaim Avraham was the son of the Alter Rebbe and was known for his striking physical resemblance to his father. He was a man of few words with an aura of spiritual grace. After the Mitteler Rebbe's passing, he refused the leadership and insisted his nephew the Tzemach Tzedek take the mantle.

Rabbi Moshe was the youngest son of the Alter Rebbe, born in 1779. He was remarkably brilliant, and his father closely guided his studies in both revealed and esoteric Torah. He accompanied his father to Petersburg to assist with communal affairs and advocacy.

Rebbetzin Sheina was the wife of the Mitteler Rebbe and the daughter of a Chassidic melamed. She was intimately involved in the inner life of the Rebbe's household and witnessed many spiritual events. She raised her orphaned granddaughter, who later married the Rebbe Maharash.

Rabbi Shalom Shachna was the father of the Tzemach Tzedek and son of Rabbi Noach. He was chosen by the Alter Rebbe to marry his daughter Devorah Leah. Following his wife's tragic passing, their son was raised by the Alter Rebbe.

common_name: Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka (wife of Tzemach Tzedek)
bio: Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka was the daughter of the Mitteler Rebbe and wife of the Tzemach Tzedek. She gave birth to the Rebbe Maharash in 1834, on the 100th anniversary of the Baal Shem Tov's revelation.

Rabbi Chaim Shneur Zalman was a son of the Tzemach Tzedek who led Chassidim as the Rebbe of Liadi. He was a man of frail health but great wisdom, and thousands sought his counsel. He passed away in 1879.
Rabbi Yisrael Noach was a son of the Tzemach Tzedek known for his exceptional scholarship. Following his father's passing, he led a Chassidic court in Nezhin.

Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak was a son of the Tzemach Tzedek who became the Rebbe in Ovruch. He was the father of Rebbetzin Shterna Sara. He served as a spiritual guide to the Chassidim in the Volhynia region.
Rabbi Yaakov was a son of the Tzemach Tzedek. He is mentioned in historical records alongside his brothers in the lineage of the Chabad Rebbes.

Rebbetzin Rivka was the wife of the Rebbe Maharash and daughter of R. Aharon of Shklov. Orphaned at a young age, she was raised by her grandmother Rebbetzin Sheina. She was known for her immense charity and passed away on 10 Shevat 1914.

Rabbi Zalman Aharon, known as Raza, was the elder son of the Rebbe Maharash. Although highly gifted in Chassidic thought, he declined to assume the mantle of leadership, allowing his younger brother to become Rebbe. He engaged in business to support his family.

Rabbi Menachem Mendel was the youngest son of the Rebbe Maharash. He later moved to Paris and then to Corsica, where he lived out his life. The Rebbe visited him in Paris to bring him closer to the Chabad leadership.

Rebbetzin Nechama Dina was the wife of the Rebbe Rayatz and daughter of R. Avraham Schneerson of Kishinev. She was deeply devoted to her husband and shared in the hardships of his leadership during Soviet persecution and their subsequent exiles.
Sheina Horenshtein was the youngest daughter of the Rebbe Rayatz. She was born in 1904. She and her husband remained in Poland and perished in Treblinka during the Holocaust in 1942.
Chana Gurary was the eldest daughter of the Rebbe Rayatz.

Rabbi Shmaryahu Gurary, known as Rashag, was the son-in-law of the Rebbe Rayatz. He served as the executive director of the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva network and directed its fundraising and administration in America.

Rabbi Menachem Mendel Horenshtein was the son-in-law of the Rebbe Rayatz, married to his youngest daughter Sheina. He was trapped in Otwock, Poland during World War II. He tragically perished in Treblinka in 1942.

Rabbi Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk was a senior disciple of the Maggid of Mezritch. He led the first major Chassidic Aliyah to the Holy Land. After his departure, he instructed his followers to accept the Alter Rebbe's leadership.

Rabbi Avraham of Kalisk was a leading Chassidic master and colleague of the Alter Rebbe. He traveled to the Maggid of Mezritch for guidance and later immigrated to the Holy Land. He maintained correspondence with the Alter Rebbe regarding the spiritual and financial needs of the Holy Land settlers.

Rabbi Levi Yitzchak of Berditchev was a famous Chassidic master and close colleague of the Alter Rebbe. He constantly advocated for the Jewish people before the Heavenly Court. He married into the Alter Rebbe's family at the 'Great Wedding' in Zhlobin.

journey.event: Served as Rabbi and Chassidic leader

bio: Rabbi Yisrael of Ruzhin was a prominent Chassidic Rebbe who maintained close ties with Chabad leaders. After being imprisoned in Russia, he relocated to Sadigura in the Austrian Empire. He lived a life of royal bearing while remaining profoundly holy.

Rabbi Shlomo of Karlin was a major Chassidic leader and a colleague of the Alter Rebbe. Once, when he visited Liozna, the Alter Rebbe sent his sons to welcome him outside the city, remarking on his exceptional spiritual stature.

Rabbi Shmuel Munkes was a legendary elder Chassid known for his fervent devotion to the Alter Rebbe. He masked his deep piety with sharp humor and unconventional antics. He was a beloved fixture in the Rebbe's court and frequently interacted with the young Mitteler Rebbe.

journey.event: Became a devoted Chassid of the Alter Rebbe

bio: Rabbi Baruch Mordechai of Bobruisk was an eminent Chassid and Torah scholar. He faced significant opposition in Vilna before becoming a central figure in the Alter Rebbe's court. After serving as Rabbi in Bobruisk for fifty years, he immigrated to Jerusalem.

journey.event: Suffered persecution for his Chassidic leanings

bio: Rabbi David Tzvi Chen, known as Radatz, was a prominent Chabad Rabbi and halachic authority in Chernigov. He was a deeply respected elder Chassid who frequently consulted with the Rebbe Rashab.
Rabbi Peretz Chen was a major Chabad figure and Rabbi in Chernigov. He was a descendant of a prominent Chassidic family and maintained close connections with the Chabad Rebbes.
Rabbi Meir Simcha Chen was a renowned Chassid and scholar. He lived in Nevel and his daughter married a prominent Chabad Rabbi.

journey.event: Lived and taught as a Chassid

bio: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Chen served as a Rabbi in Nezhin. He was an active and respected figure within the Chabad community.

Rabbi Shmuel Betzalel Sheftel, known as Rashbatz, was a revered teacher and mentor to the Rebbe Rayatz. He began teaching the Rayatz in 1893 and guided him in his early studies of Chassidus. He later served as a leading educator in Tomchei Temimim.

journey.event: Appointed tutor for the young Rebbe Rayatz

bio: Rabbi Chanoch Hendel Kugel was an essential mashpia and mentor in Lubavitch. The Rebbe Rashab valued his Chassidic stories and his ability to transmit the traditions of elder Chassidim.
Rabbi Aba Person of Chashnik was a notable Chassid and storyteller. His recollections were highly valued by the Rebbe Rashab, who enjoyed hearing his authentic Chabad traditions.
Rabbi Meir Simcha Chen was a renowned Chassid and scholar. He lived in Nevel, and his family was deeply connected to the Chabad leadership.

journey.event: Lived and taught as a Chassid

bio: Rabbi Aba Person of Chashnik was a notable Chassid and storyteller. His precise recollections of early Chabad history were highly valued by the Rebbe Rashab.

Rabbi Shmuel Dovber Lipkin of Borisov, known as Rashdam, was an intellectual Chassid and prominent figure. He was deeply respected by the Rebbes and frequently engaged in profound Chassidic discussions with the Rebbe Rashab.
Rabbi Yaakov Mordechai Bezpalov served as the Rabbi of Poltava and was a deeply respected Chassid. He was a leading figure during the eras of the Rebbe Maharash and Rebbe Rashab.
Rabbi Yosef Tumarkin was a distinguished Rabbi of Kremenchug. He was an eminent Chabad chassid who maintained a close connection with the Tzemach Tzedek.
Rabbi Aharon Tumarkin was a prominent Chabad Rabbi who served in Kharkov. He profoundly influenced the spiritual life of the city and was known for his erudition and hospitality.
Rabbi Yitzchak Dubov was a respected Chassid and mashpia. He was actively involved in guiding students in the ways of Chassidus.

Rabbi Shmuel Levitin was a leading Chabad Rabbi who initially served in Rakshik, Lithuania. He was sent by the Rebbe Rayatz as an emissary to the United States, where he became a major figure in spreading Chassidus.

Rabbi Eliyahu Simpson was a trusted Chassid and member of the Rebbe Rayatz's administration. He served as a Rabbi in New York from 1923 and was a founding member of Agudas HaTemimim and an active leader in Agudas Chasidei Chabad.

Rabbi Yisrael Jacobson was a central figure in establishing Chabad in the United States. After emigrating from Russia in 1926, he founded Agudas HaTemimim, served as an administrator for Agudas Chasidei Chabad, and helped establish the Beis Rivkah school network.

Rabbi Mordechai Dubin was a Latvian politician and parliament member who worked tirelessly for the Jewish community. He was deeply devoted to the Rebbe Rayatz, playing a crucial role in securing his release from Soviet imprisonment, and accompanied the Rebbe to the United States in 1930.
Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Slonim was a leading figure of the Chabad community in Hebron. He served as a community leader and managed the affairs of Kollel Chabad in the Holy City.
Rabbi Yaakov Kuli Slonim was a significant member of the Slonim family in Hebron. He was the esteemed son-in-law of the Mitteler Rebbe.
Rabbi Mordechai Dovber Slonim served as a community leader for Chabad in Hebron. He was a grandson of the Mitteler Rebbe and an important figure in the local community.

Rabbi Shimon Menashe Chaikin was a venerated Rabbi and leader of the Chabad community in Hebron. Originally from Shklov, he settled in Hebron and served as a prominent halachic authority and administrator for Kollel Chabad.

journey.event: Served as Rabbi and halachic authority

bio: Rabbi Chaim Eliezer Bichovsky was a leading Chassid who published many foundational Chabad texts. He was a dedicated publisher and helped preserve important Chassidic manuscripts for future generations.

Rabbi Shlomo Zalman Havlin founded and led the Toras Emes yeshiva in Hebron and Jerusalem. He was sent by the Rebbe Rashab to the Holy Land to establish the yeshiva and served as its primary mashpia and administrator.
Rabbi Chanoch Hendel Havlin was instrumental in the Chabad community's efforts in the Holy Land. He was heavily involved in community activism and supporting the Chabad institutions in Jerusalem.
Rabbi Chaim Futerfas was a dedicated member of the Chabad underground in Russia. He worked tirelessly to preserve and sustain Jewish practice under the oppressive Soviet regime.

Rabbi Yitzchak Horowitz, famously known as Itche Der Masmid, was an exceptionally devoted Chassid. He traveled widely as an emissary to strengthen Judaism before being tragically murdered in the Holocaust.

Rabbi Moshe Leib Rodstein, originally named Zusia Shifrin, served as a secretary to the Rebbe in Poland. Upon arriving in the United States in late 1940, he was sent to direct the Agudas Chasidei Chabad in Chicago before returning in 1946 to serve as a secretary for the Rebbe.

journey.event: Returned to serve as the Rebbe's secretary

bio: Rabbi Nissan Telushkin was a distinguished Rabbi and a prominent halachic authority in the United States. He was an active and respected figure in the rabbinic leadership of the American Chabad community.

journey.event: Served as a distinguished Rabbi and halachic authority

bio: Rabbi Avraham Eliyahu Axelrod was a leading Rabbi in Baltimore and a dedicated supporter of the Rebbe Rayatz. After arriving in the United States in 1924, he played a crucial role in establishing and maintaining the Chabad community in his city.

journey.event: Served as Rabbi for the local Chabad community

bio: Rabbi Moshe Axelrod was a dedicated Rabbi in Russia who valiantly maintained his religious activities under oppressive Soviet rule. He served as the Rabbi of Surazh and repeatedly sought ways to obtain exit visas for himself and his family.

journey.event: Served as Rabbi under Soviet rule

bio: Rabbi Shraga Feivish Zalmanov was a notable activist in the Chabad community who operated out of Warsaw. He served as a crucial conduit for transferring relief funds sent from the United States to support Chabad institutions in Soviet Russia.

journey.event: Facilitated relief funds for Russian Jewry

bio: Rabbi Chaim Mordechai Aizik Hodakov was the chief of staff and head of the secretariat for the Rebbe. Arriving in the United States with the Rebbe Rayatz in the winter of 1940, he was appointed director of the Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch and Machne Israel.

Rabbi Chaim Liberman served as the personal secretary and dedicated librarian for the Rebbe Rayatz. Due to his steadfast involvement with the Rebbe's underground network, he was arrested by the Soviets and exiled to Tambov.

journey.event: Arrested and exiled by Soviet authorities

bio: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Feldman was a dedicated Chassid and a prominent Torah scholar who assisted the Rebbe Rayatz's efforts. Known for his exceptional brilliance, he served as a respected Rabbi in Baltimore.
Mr. Chaim Shlomo Palmer was a dedicated philanthropist and activist based in Chicago. He consistently supported Chabad institutions and was highly active in the Agudas Chasidei Chabad.

journey.event: Supported Chabad institutions as a community leader

bio: Rabbi Yosef Weinberg was a prominent activist who played an essential role in building Chabad in America. He is famously known for broadcasting Chassidic teachings on the radio, greatly expanding the reach of the movement.

Rabbi Yaakov Yosef of Polonne was a leading disciple of the Baal Shem Tov and the author of the foundational text Toldot Yaakov Yosef. He was the very first to publish Chassidic teachings in print, extensively citing and explaining the words of his master.

Rabbi Pinchas of Koretz was a prominent early Chassidic master and a close colleague of the Baal Shem Tov. He was highly revered for his immense piety, his focus on the attribute of truth, and his central role in the early spread of the movement.

Rabbi Yechiel Michel of Zlotchov was a major disciple of both the Baal Shem Tov and the Maggid of Mezritch. He was an influential early master who guided many of the subsequent leaders of the Chassidic movement through his fiery devotion and teachings.

Rabbi Meshulam Zusia of Anipoli was a famous, deeply pious Chassidic master and a beloved disciple of the Maggid of Mezritch. Known for his profound humility and simple devotion, he remains one of the most endearing and legendary figures in Chassidic history.

Rabbi Elimelech of Lizhensk was a leading disciple of the Maggid of Mezritch and the author of the classic text Noam Elimelech. He was the primary architect of the Chassidic movement in Poland, establishing the archetype of the Chassidic Tzaddik.

Rabbi Shmuel Shmelke of Nikolsburg was a prominent disciple of the Maggid of Mezritch and an influential leader who served as the Chief Rabbi of Moravia. Together with his brother, Rabbi Pinchas Horowitz, he brought the fervor of Chassidism to the mainstream rabbinic world.

Rabbi Pinchas Horowitz was a devoted disciple of the Maggid of Mezritch and the author of the monumental Talmudic work Haflaa. Serving as the Chief Rabbi of Frankfurt, he was a staunch defender of traditional Judaism and an early pioneer of Chassidic thought in Western Europe.

Rabbi Ze'ev Wolf of Zhitomir was a leading disciple of the Maggid of Mezritch and the revered author of the Or HaMeir. His teachings helped shape the spiritual and intellectual foundations of early Chassidism.

Rabbi Aharon the Great of Karlin was an early, passionate Chassidic master and a disciple of the Maggid of Mezritch. He was instrumental in spreading the Chassidic movement throughout Lithuania before his untimely passing.

Rabbi Menachem Nachum of Chernobyl was a disciple of both the Baal Shem Tov and the Maggid of Mezritch. He was the author of the classic Chassidic work Meor Einayim and established the foundation for the Chernobyl Chassidic dynasty.

Rabbi Mordechai of Chernobyl was a prominent Chassidic Rebbe and the son of Rabbi Menachem Nachum. He significantly expanded the Chernobyl dynasty, becoming a highly influential figure who maintained ties with the leaders of Chabad.

Rabbi Baruch of Medzhybizh was the grandson of the Baal Shem Tov and an early, assertive Chassidic leader. He held court with regal bearing in Medzhybizh and was a major, sometimes controversial, authority in the emerging Chassidic world.
Rabbi Chaim of Krasna was an esteemed early disciple of the Baal Shem Tov. He was an influential figure in the very first generation of the Chassidic movement's spread.

Rabbi Leib Sarah's was a renowned hidden tzaddik and a contemporary of the early Chabad Rebbes. Wandering across Eastern Europe, he devoted his life to mystical intercession, ransoming Jewish captives, and supporting hidden scholars.

Rabbi Yissachar Dov of Lubavitch was an early disciple of the Maggid of Mezritch. He established a Chassidic presence in the town of Lubavitch years before the Alter Rebbe's successors made it the movement's capital.
Rabbi Shlomo of Usha was a prominent Chassid in the early generations of the Chabad movement. He was deeply devoted to the teachings of the Alter Rebbe and served as a spiritual guide in his region.

Rabbi Avraham, known as the "Malach" (Angel), was the son of the Maggid of Mezritch. He was celebrated for his extreme asceticism and otherworldly piety, completely detaching himself from physical desires.

Rabbi Shalom Shachna of Prohovitch was the son of Rabbi Avraham the Malach and the father of the majestic Rabbi Yisrael of Ruzhin. He served as a bridge between the intense asceticism of his father and the regal Chassidic courts established by his son.

Rabbi Aharon of Strashelye was the brilliant and most prominent disciple of the Alter Rebbe. After his master's passing, he emphasized explosive emotional ecstasy in prayer, eventually forming his own independent Chassidic following in Strashelye in ideological contrast to the Mitteler Rebbe.

Rabbi Yitzchak Goldin was a prominent Chassid who personally assisted the Rebbe Rashab during his final illness in Rostov. Arrested in 1932 for his involvement in the Chabad educational underground, he survived a commuted death sentence and exile in Kazakhstan before escaping to the United States in 1946.

Rabbi Avraham Baruch Pevzner was a highly regarded Mashpia and devoted Chassid in Soviet Russia. He played a critical role in sustaining the clandestine yeshiva network before being arrested and ultimately sacrificing his life for his religious activities.

Rabbi Yona Eidelkop was a dedicated Chabad activist who worked tirelessly to spread Yiddishkeit and Chassidic teachings. Operating in highly challenging environments, he was a central figure in organizing secret Jewish education.

Rabbi Moshe Rosenblum was an important chronicler of Chabad history. He is noted for his role in recording and preserving the legacy of the movement through his work Toldot HaTemimim, detailing the lives of the early students of the Lubavitch yeshiva.

Rabbi Yehuda Chitrik was a highly respected elder Chassid and the author of the biographical series Reshimot Devarim. Living to be over a hundred years old, he served as a living bridge to the movement's past, meticulously recording and transmitting the oral history of the Chabad Rebbes.

Rabbi Tzvi Chitrik was a devoted Chassid and the father of Rabbi Yehuda Chitrik. He maintained a strong connection to the Chabad leadership and instilled a deep love for Chassidic tradition, scholarship, and storytelling in his family.

Rabbi Yaakov Yisrael Zuber was a prominent Chabad Rabbi who initially served as an emissary in Soviet Georgia to strengthen local Jewish communities. After escaping the Soviet Union, he served as a rabbi in Stockholm, Sweden, before ultimately immigrating to the United States.

Rabbi Zalman Shimon Dvorkin was a deeply respected Torah scholar and Chassid. He served for many decades as the central halachic authority (Mara D'Asra) for the Crown Heights Chabad community, guiding thousands with his extensive halachic expertise.

Rabbi Saadia Liberov was a self-sacrificing Chassid and dedicated Shliach who devoted his life to the Rebbe's outreach efforts. He worked tirelessly to establish and strengthen Jewish infrastructure, ultimately serving as a central pillar of the Chabad community in Antwerp, Belgium.

Rabbi Yaakov Eizik Blattner was a devoted Chassid who meticulously documented many Chassidic stories and memoirs. His writings helped preserve the atmosphere and oral traditions of the early Chabad generations for posterity.

Rabbi Leib (Aryeh Leib) Eidelman was a dedicated Chassid from the town of Gzhatsk. He remained fiercely loyal to the Chabad Rebbes and maintained a steadfast commitment to Chassidic study and practice throughout his life.

Rabbi Leib Cohen was a respected Chassid and the brother of the famous activist Reb Refael Nachman Cohen. He was deeply embedded in the Chabad community, upholding the movement's rigorous spiritual traditions during a tumultuous era.

Rabbi Tovia Belkin was an esteemed Chassidic elder and a vital relayer of foundational Chabad traditions. His recorded memoirs and stories provided an authentic glimpse into the lives and teachings of the early Rebbes and their disciples.

Rabbi Efraim Wolff was a central Chabad activist and the administrative head of the Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva network in Israel. Working closely under the Rebbe's directives, he was instrumental in expanding the movement's educational footprint in the Holy Land.

Rabbi Moshe Yitzchak Hecht was a prominent and energetic Shliach of the Rebbe. Sent to New Haven, Connecticut, he successfully established a flourishing Jewish day school and transformed the local community's religious landscape.

Rabbi Yaakov Yehuda Hecht was a famous Chabad Rabbi, a charismatic orator, and the executive vice president of the National Committee for Furtherance of Jewish Education (NCFJE). He was a defining voice for Chabad in America, translating the Rebbe's campaigns into massive public action.

Rabbi Avraham Dov Hecht was a prominent Chabad Rabbi who led a major Syrian Jewish congregation in Brooklyn. He was highly respected for his rabbinic leadership and served as the president of the Rabbinical Alliance of America.

Rabbi Yochanan Gordon was a highly respected Gabbai and a dedicated Chassid in the Rebbe's court at 770 Eastern Parkway. He faithfully managed the synagogue's affairs and the Rebbe's minyanim for many years.

Rabbi Sholom Dovber Gordon was a dedicated Chassid and an active Shliach. He worked energetically in the United States to promote Chabad's educational initiatives, youth programs, and communal outreach.

Rabbi Mordechai Mentlik was the venerated Rosh Yeshiva of the central Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva at 770 in Brooklyn. Known for his profound Talmudic scholarship and fiery Chassidic warmth, he educated generations of prominent Chabad rabbis and emissaries.

journey.event: Appointed Rosh Yeshiva of Tomchei Temimim

bio: Rabbi Dovid Raskin was a prominent Chassid and the long-time chairman of the Lubavitch Youth Organization in New York. He meticulously directed the Rebbe's outreach campaigns and managed the daily operations of the central Chabad yeshiva.

Rabbi Sholom Mendel Kalmanson was a deeply dedicated Chassid and an influential Shliach. Entrusted with crucial rescue missions by the Rebbe Rayatz and the Rebbe, he worked to sustain Jewish refugees and later directed Chabad activities in Europe.

Rabbi Binyamin Gorodetzky was the Rebbe's chief representative and personal emissary in Europe and North Africa. Operating from the Chabad bureau in Paris, he masterminded the establishment of extensive educational networks across Morocco, France, and Israel.

Rabbi Yisrael of Polotsk was an early leader of the Chassidic Aliyah to the Holy Land. He traveled alongside Rabbi Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk to establish the Chassidic settlement in Tiberias, serving as a critical bridge between the movement in Europe and the diaspora in the Land of Israel.

Rabbi Yeshaya of Yanovitch was a deeply devoted early Chassid of the Alter Rebbe. He served as a crucial figure in the dissemination of the early Chabad teachings and maintained a steadfast loyalty to the Rebbe's court.

Rabbi Avraham Landau of Zembin was a prominent Chassid and profound scholar. He was highly regarded by the Alter Rebbe and subsequent generations for his exceptional piety, deep Torah knowledge, and pure Chassidic devotion.

Rabbi Ziskind of Kurenets was a respected elder Chassid in the court of the Alter Rebbe. He is frequently mentioned in early Chabad lore as an archetype of sincere devotion, prayerful intensity, and unshakeable faith.

Rabbi Efraim of Vitebsk was an esteemed disciple of the Maggid of Mezritch and a close colleague of the Alter Rebbe. He played a significant role in establishing the early foundations of Chassidism in the region of White Russia.

Rabbi Efraim Zalman was a prominent early Chassid who deeply absorbed the Alter Rebbe's teachings. He guided his local community in Beshenkovitz with intense spiritual dedication and loyalty to the Chabad path.

Rabbi Yaakov Koppel Seligson was a well-known Chassidic figure from the early generations of the movement. He was celebrated for his immense dedication to the Rebbes and his refined, aristocratic character.

Rabbi Eliyahu Abeler was a devoted Chassid widely known for his profound piety and simple, unshakeable faith. He embodied the classical Chabad ideal of internalizing esoteric teachings into practical, heartfelt devotion.

Rabbi Aharon Zelig was a dedicated early Chassid who traveled from the town of Ula to learn from the Chabad masters. He was known for his rigorous spiritual discipline and absolute adherence to the movement's ethos.

name_en: Rabbi Avraham Meir the Wagon Driver
bio: Rabbi Avraham Meir was a well-known, beloved Chassidic figure during the era of the early Rebbes. Despite his simple profession as a wagon driver, he possessed a deeply refined soul and embodied the Chassidic ideal of finding God in all mundane activities.

Rabbi Betzalel Wilshansky was a prominent Rabbi and deeply devoted Chassid. He is remembered for authoring significant memoirs that chronicled the rich life, oral traditions, and history of the Chabad movement in Eastern Europe.

Rabbi Chaim Moshe of Shklov was an early Chassid and rigorous scholar. Emerging from the intellectual and often anti-Chassidic stronghold of Shklov, he became a passionately devoted follower of the Alter Rebbe's path.
Rabbi Dovber Moshe was a respected early Chassidic figure residing in Disna. He functioned as a spiritual guide in his region, actively transmitting the classical Chabad ethos to his community.
Rabbi Eliyahu Shmuel was a dedicated early Chassid deeply attached to the Alter Rebbe. He helped establish a strong and enduring Chabad presence in the town of Rogatchov.

Rabbi Mordechai of Lieply and Vitebsk was a leading Chassidic disciple of the Alter Rebbe. He served as a major spiritual influence, embodying the rigorous intellectual and emotional demands of the new Chabad path.

Rabbi Yosef Moshe of Minsk was a prominent early Chassid. Operating in a city known for fierce opposition to the movement, he remained unyieldingly loyal to the Alter Rebbe and courageously spread his teachings.
Rabbi Shlomo Chaim of Horodok was a deeply respected Chassid from the early generations. He was a devoted follower who helped anchor and expand the movement's influence in the town of Horodok.

Rabbi Moshe Eliyahu of Smilovitz was a notable early Chassidic figure. He was known for his profound spiritual connection to the Chabad masters and his role in supporting the growth of Chassidism in his area.

Rabbi Alexander Ziskind was a respected early Chassid who absorbed the foundational teachings of the Alter Rebbe. He stood as a loyal and scholarly disciple in the crucial Chassidic center of Vitebsk.

Rabbi Yitzchak Isaac Jaffe was an early Chassid and instrumental printer. He played a significant role in publishing the foundational texts of the Chabad movement, making the esoteric teachings accessible to a wider audience.

Rabbi Yisrael Jaffe, famously known as Yisrael Kozik, was the renowned Chassidic printer of Kopust. He worked directly under the Alter Rebbe's guidance to publish the first editions of the Tanya and other major Chabad works before eventually immigrating to the Holy Land.
Rabbi Yosef of Beshenkovitz was an early Chassidic figure who was deeply embedded in the foundational network of the Alter Rebbe's followers, serving as a loyal disciple throughout his life.

Rabbi Yekutiel Zalman of Vilna was a respected early Chassid and the grandson of the renowned Rabbi Levi Yitzchak of Berditchev. He married into the Chabad dynasty as the son-in-law of the Mitteler Rebbe.

Rabbi Yaakov of Smilyan was a fiercely devoted Chassid. He regularly traveled long distances to the courts of the Chabad Rebbes to absorb their teachings and internalize the profound ethos of the movement.

Rabbi Reuven Sofer was a trusted scribe (Sofer) for the Alter Rebbe, renowned for his intense, otherworldly holiness. He meticulously wrote Torah scrolls and tefillin infused with profound kabbalistic meditations.

Rabbi Meir Refaels was a highly prominent and wealthy Chassid based in Vilna. He endured significant personal and financial persecution during the intense opposition to Chassidism but remained unshakeably loyal to the Alter Rebbe.

Rabbi Yeshaya Hakohen Shapiro was a prominent Chassid and scholar. He served during the later generations of Chabad in Russia, actively preserving the rich intellectual and spiritual heritage of the movement.

Rabbi Avraham David Shapiro was a deeply respected Chassid. He was intimately involved in the communal and spiritual struggles of the Chabad community as they sought to maintain tradition in Eastern Europe.
Rabbi Yitzchak Rubinstein was a leading Chabad Rabbi. He provided critical religious leadership and halachic guidance to his community during the tumultuous years of the early twentieth century.

Rabbi Mordechai Elinson was a devoted Chassidic figure noted especially for his extraordinary hospitality. He dedicated his life to the welfare of his fellow Chassidim, maintaining a warm and open home.

Rabbi Yisrael Neta Gnesin was a well-known Chassid and scholar. He played an active role in the intellectual and religious life of the community as it navigated the severe challenges of the Soviet era.

Rabbi Yehoshua Eliyahu Volosov was a prominent and highly active Chassid. He was deeply involved in the administration and financial support of the Chabad yeshiva system during its most fragile years in Eastern Europe.

Rabbi Yaakov Utevsky was a completely devoted Chassid. He maintained a steadfast commitment to traditional Judaism and supported the Chabad underground during the darkest and most dangerous years of Communist rule.

Rabbi Hillel Levin was a respected and deeply learned Chassid. He faithfully carried the movement's classical traditions and teachings through the massive societal upheavals of the early twentieth century.
Rabbi Zeev Wolf Steingart was a prominent Chassid in the Chabad movement. He dedicated himself to the spread of Chassidic teachings and provided strong support to his community.
Rabbi Shmuel Steingart was a respected Chassid known for his deep piety. He maintained a steadfast adherence to the spiritual path of Chabad during challenging times.
Rabbi Uziel Chazanov was a devoted Chassid. He faced the hardships of his generation with steadfast faith and a deep attachment to the Chabad Rebbes.
Rabbi Betzalel Rubashkin was a prominent Chassid. He was an active figure who supported the movement's institutions and remained deeply connected to Chabad leadership.
Rabbi Yehuda Botrashvili was a respected Chassid. He was known for his dedication to the Rebbe and his active efforts to strengthen Jewish life and education.
Rabbi Natan Gurary was a prominent and wealthy Chassid. He utilized his considerable resources to financially support Chabad institutions and the vital activities of the Rebbes.
Rabbi Chaim Yosef Rosenblum was an active Chassid closely connected to the Rebbe Rayatz. He assisted the Rebbe in various critical communal and underground efforts in Eastern Europe.
Rabbi Shimon Berliner was a respected Chassid. As a descendant of earlier Chabad luminaries, he carried forward a rich familial legacy of scholarship and unyielding devotion.

Mendel Beilis was a famous historical figure who became the center of a notorious anti-Semitic blood libel trial in Russia. During his harrowing ordeal, he was strongly defended and supported by the Chabad Rebbes and the broader Jewish community.

Rabbi Yaakov Maskalik, also known as Yaakov Zuravitzer, was a self-sacrificing Chassid and emissary. He courageously undertook dangerous missions to support and sustain Jewish life in the Soviet underground.
Rabbi Sholom Dovber Notik was a dedicated Chassid and chronicler. He played an important role in transcribing and preserving many oral Chassidic stories and historical traditions.
Rabbi Shmuel Krugliak was a prominent Chassid who served with distinction as a Gabbai. He helped manage the daily affairs and functions of the synagogue with great dedication.
Rabbi Tzvi Hirsch Rivkin of Klimovitch was a devoted Chassid. He steadfastly adhered to the teachings of the Rebbes and maintained a strong Chassidic presence in his hometown.
Rabbi Simcha of Beshenkovitz was a famous Chassidic storyteller. His rich narratives and deep recollections helped transmit the atmosphere and teachings of early Chabad to subsequent generations.
Rabbi Akiva Menuhin was a respected Chassid. He was deeply committed to the movement's ideals and served as a loyal follower during an era of significant transition and challenge.
Rabbi Aharon Khazanov was a devoted Chassid. He actively participated in the Chabad community's dedicated efforts to sustain religious life under oppressive circumstances.
Rabbi Peretz Motzkin was a legendary Mashpia and Chassid. He was known for his intense dedication and played a major role in guiding the spiritual lives of his students in the later generations.
Rabbi Aryeh Leib Sheinin was a respected Chassid and scholar. He contributed greatly to the intellectual and spiritual vibrancy of the Chabad community in Eastern Europe.

Rabbi Yaakov Yehuda Rizek was a self-sacrificing Rabbi who courageously maintained Jewish observance in Soviet Russia. He was arrested for his religious activities and tragically died in Soviet imprisonment.
Rabbi Shamai Vigan was a dedicated Chassid. He remained deeply loyal to the Rebbes and actively supported the community's spiritual and underground initiatives.
Rabbi Zalman David Michlin was a prominent Chassidic figure. He was deeply involved in communal affairs and dedicated to the preservation of authentic Chabad traditions.
Rabbi Hillel Jochvedin was an active Chassid in Soviet Russia. He bravely assisted the clandestine Tomchei Temimim yeshiva students, providing them with essential material and spiritual support.
Rabbi Chaim Jochvedin was a dedicated Chassid who, alongside his family, worked selflessly to sustain the underground Jewish network in Eastern Europe.
Rabbi Yehoshua Korf was a devoted Chassid and an influential Mashpia. He was a well-known figure who guided many students and community members in the authentic ways of Chassidus and prayer.

Rabbi Dovid Shifrin was an active Chabad leader and secretary for Agudas Chasidei Chabad in the United States. He worked energetically to organize and build the movement's American infrastructure prior to and during the arrival of the Rebbe Rayatz.

Rabbi Avraham Shmuel Heilman was a pioneering Chabad historian and the author of Beit Rabbi. This monumental work served as the first comprehensive biographical history of the early Chabad Rebbes and their movement.
Rabbi Alexander Sender Nemtzov was a devoted Chassid. He was firmly attached to the Chabad leadership and contributed to the community's spiritual resilience.

Rabbi Shmuel Isaac Dubin was the brother of the famous Latvian politician Mordechai Dubin. He was a dedicated activist who supported his brother's extensive efforts on behalf of European Jewry and the Chabad Rebbes.
Rabbi Eliyahu Chaim Althaus was a devoted Chassid and activist. He was deeply connected to the Chabad Rebbes and frequently corresponded with the Rebbe Rayatz regarding communal affairs.
Rabbi Menachem Ze'ev Greenglass was a leading Mashpia and Rabbi in Montreal. He was a central figure in establishing and guiding the Chabad community and Yeshiva in Canada.
Rabbi Yitzchak Dovid Groner was a leading Shliach and Rabbi in Australia. He dedicated his life to building Jewish infrastructure and Chabad institutions across the continent.
Rabbi Shalom Posner was a prominent Chabad Rabbi and Shliach in the United States. He was deeply involved in Jewish education and outreach efforts.
Rabbi Yehoshua Mondshine was a preeminent Chabad historian, researcher, and author. He dedicated his life to uncovering and publishing authentic Chassidic manuscripts and historical documents.

journey.event: Served as a leading Chabad historian and researcher

bio: Rabbi Avraham Chanoch Glitzenstein was a respected author and compiler. He is best known for authoring the Sefer HaToldot series and Otzar Sipurei Chabad, vital works documenting Chabad history.

journey.event: Compiled and authored major works of Chabad history

bio: Rabbi Avraham David Pozner of Klimovitch was a prominent Chassid during the era of the Rebbe Rashab. He was deeply respected for his piety and dedication to Chassidus.
Rabbi Chaim Yona Lutski was a dedicated Chassid known for his spiritual commitment and connection to the Chabad leadership.
Rabbi Tzvi Hirsch Azimov of Klimovitch was a prominent Chassid. He was an active and respected figure within his local Chabad community.
Rabbi Yisrael Noach Belinitzki was a prominent activist in the Chabad underground in Russia. He worked tirelessly under dangerous conditions to maintain Jewish education and religious practice.
Rabbi Isser Klobgant was an active Chabad supporter and activist. He was deeply involved in assisting the community during difficult times in Soviet Russia.
Rabbi Chaikel Chanin was a dedicated Chassid who remained steadfast in his religious commitment despite the hardships of his era.
Rabbi Yisrael Leibov was a devoted activist in the Soviet era. He worked to preserve Jewish traditions and support the underground Chassidic network.
Rabbi Chaim Minkowitz was an active Chassid and administrator. He played a significant role in managing Chabad institutions and assisting the community.
Rabbi Shmuel Betzalel Althaus was a devoted Chassid and a prominent community figure. He actively participated in the life of the Chabad community and was well-respected among his peers.

Rabbi Shlomo Zalman Hecht was a leading Shliach of the Rebbe who spearheaded Chabad's activities in Chicago. He spent decades expanding Jewish education and outreach programs across the Midwestern United States.

Rabbi Shlomo of Krichev was a devoted and elderly Chassid frequently mentioned with deep reverence in the Alter Rebbe's letters. He was a trusted elder who guided the younger generation with his profound wisdom.

Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson of Repka was a respected descendant of the Chabad Rebbes. Serving his community in Repka, he faced severe hardships and eventual exile while striving to uphold his aristocratic spiritual heritage.

Rabbi Yisrael Novik was an immensely dedicated Chassid. He worked courageously within the Chabad underground network to preserve Jewish life, education, and practice under the oppressive Soviet regime.

Rabbi Shneur Zalman Schneerson was a prominent Rabbinic figure from the illustrious Schneerson family. He served as a distinguished community leader and spiritual guide in the city of Lodz prior to the destruction of the community.

Rabbi Menachem Mendel Deitsch was a dedicated Chassidic figure known for his extensive communal activism. He worked tirelessly to sustain Jewish education and religious practice despite the continuous threat of imprisonment.
Rabbi Shmuel Leib Levin of Paritch was a known intellectual Chassid. He was recognized for his depth of thought and his profound devotion to Chabad teachings.
Rabbi Shmuel Katzman was a devoted Chabad activist. He played a significant role in organizing and supporting communal outreach initiatives for the movement.
Rabbi Shmuel Bezpalov was a respected Chassid. As part of a distinguished Rabbinic family, he maintained the deep scholarly and spiritual traditions of Chabad in his generation.
Rabbi Yehuda Leib Raskin was a respected Chassid and Shliach. He actively participated in the Rebbe's campaigns to spread Judaism.
Rabbi Yaakov Katz was an active Chabad leader and supporter in the United States. He was instrumental in assisting the Rebbes and supporting the expansion of Chabad institutions.
Rabbi Meir Ashkenazi served as the Chabad Chief Rabbi of Shanghai. During World War II, he played a vital role in assisting and providing for the spiritual needs of Jewish refugees who fled to China.

Rabbi Shalom Dovber Levin serves as the chief librarian of the central Chabad library. He is the principal editor of the Igrot Kodesh series and has published numerous historical works and diaries of the Rebbes.

journey.event: Appointed chief librarian of the Chabad library

bio: Rabbi Zalman Schneerson of Velizh was a respected descendant of the Chabad Rebbes. He maintained the Chassidic legacy of his family as a dedicated Chassid.

journey.event: Lived as a respected descendant and Chassid

bio: Rabbi Shmuel Gurary of Kremenchug was a major philanthropist and a devoted Chassid. He heavily supported the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva and assisted the Rebbe Rashab in numerous communal affairs.

journey.event: Supported Tomchei Temimim as a major philanthropist

bio: Rabbi Nechemia of Dubrovna was a leading halachic authority and the author of the work Divrei Nechemia. He served as a Rabbi in Dubrovna and corresponded with the Chabad Rebbes.
Rabbi Aharon of Dubrovna was a prominent Chassid and scholar.
Rabbi Eliyahu Yosef Rivlin was a prominent Chassid and the Rabbi of Dribin. He is known as the author of the work Oholei Yosef.

Rabbi Shmaryahu Paitelson of Lieple was a key Chassid and translator during the time of the Tzemach Tzedek. When the Rebbe was summoned to Petersburg, he called upon Rabbi Shmaryahu to accompany him and serve as an interpreter and advocate before the government ministers.

journey.event: Accompanied the Tzemach Tzedek to advocate before the government

bio: Rabbi Yisrael Chaikin was a devoted early Chassid and translator.
Rabbi Zalman Neimark served as the prominent Rabbi of Starodub. He was an active Chabad Rabbi and a dedicated Chassid.
Rabbi Moshe Maisels was a prominent Chassid of the Alter Rebbe. He assisted the Rebbe significantly and acted as a spy for Russia against Napoleon.
Rabbi Yehuda Leib the Batlan of Dinaburg was a scholarly and dedicated Chassid of the Alter Rebbe.
Rabbi Shlomo Freides was an early prominent, wealthy Chassid and brilliant scholar of the Alter Rebbe.
Rabbi Yosef Kolbo of Shklov was a trusted Chassid of the early Rebbes.
