Rabbi Gavriel Nosse Chen was the progenitor of the distinguished Chen family in Chabad.
Rabbi Moshe Chein was a prominent Rabbi in the distinguished Chen family.
Rabbi Avraham Chein was a distinguished Rabbi, scholar, and thinker within the Chabad movement.
Rabbi Yitzchak Shaul of Klimovitch was a well-known and devoted Chassid.

Rabbi Yosef Rozen, known as the Rogatchover Gaon, was one of the greatest Talmudic geniuses of his time and served as Rabbi in Dvinsk. He maintained exceptionally strong ties to Chabad and corresponded frequently with its leaders.
Rabbi Shimon Lazarev was a prominent Chabad Rabbi who suffered under the Soviet regime.
Rabbi Shneur Zalman Vilenkin was a dedicated melamed who taught the Rebbe in his youth in Yekaterinoslav.

Rabbi Ben Tzion Shemtov was a legendary Chassid from Druya who spread Judaism under the Soviets. He tirelessly worked to sustain Chabad activities despite severe oppression and later continued his work in the UK.
Rabbi Asher Grossman was a devoted Chassid and shochet from Nikolaev.

journey.event: Served as a shochet and devoted Chassid

bio: Rabbi Shmaryahu Yehuda Leib Medalie was a prominent Chabad Rabbi who served as Chief Rabbi of Vitebsk and Moscow. He was tragically martyred by the Soviets.
Rabbi Menachem Mendel Gluskin was a respected Chabad leader who served as the Chief Rabbi of Leningrad. He guided the community through very difficult times.
Rabbi Yitzchak Yoel Rafalovitch was a prominent Chassidic figure who served as the Rabbi of Kremenchug.

Chava was the mother of Rabbi Dovber, the Maggid of Mezritch. Together with her husband Avraham, she raised the child who would eventually succeed the Baal Shem Tov and become the chief architect of the Hasidic movement.

Sara was the mother of Rabbi Yisrael Baal Shem Tov, the founder of the Hasidic movement. She and her husband Eliezer were known for their exceptional piety, humility, and unbounded hospitality toward the poor.

Rabbi Yehuda Leib Segal of Vitebsk was a wealthy and prominent scholar who became the father-in-law of the Alter Rebbe. Initially a staunch opponent of the Hasidic path his son-in-law chose, he eventually recognized the Alter Rebbe's extraordinary greatness.

Rabbi Shalom Dovber of Rechitsa was a son of the Maharil of Kopust and a grandson of the Tzemach Tzedek. He established his own Chassidic court in Rechitsa, serving as a prominent leader and spiritual guide within the Kopust branch of the movement.

Rabbi Shmaryahu Noach of Bobruisk was a prominent leader of the Kopust branch of Chabad and the author of Shemen Lamor. Following his passing in 1923, the last of the breakaway dynasties effectively ended, leaving the movement entirely united under the central Lubavitch leadership.

Rabbi Yitzchak Dovber, known as the Maharid, was a grandson of the Tzemach Tzedek who led the Chabad court in Liadi. He authored a deeply profound compilation of Chassidic discourses and guided his followers until his passing in 1910.

Rabbi Meir Shlomo Yanovsky served as the esteemed Chief Rabbi of Nikolaev. He was a devoted Chassid of the Rebbe Rashab and the maternal grandfather of the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe, deeply influencing his grandson's early environment.

Rabbi Avraham David Lavut was an outstanding halachic authority and the great-grandfather of the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe. He authored Kav Venaki and Shaar HaKolel, serving with distinction as the Rabbi of Nikolaev.

Rabbi Avraham David Yanovsky was the father of Rabbi Meir Shlomo Yanovsky. Embedded in the rich Chabad tradition, he raised his son to become the future Chief Rabbi of Nikolaev and grandfather of the seventh Rebbe.

Rabbi Shalom Shlomo Schneerson was the brother of Rabbi Levi Yitzchak and an uncle to the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe. He was a dedicated scholar and Chassidic figure who carried forward his family's distinguished rabbinic heritage.

journey.event: Passed away and was buried on the Mount of Olives

bio: Rabbi Baruch Schneur Schneerson was a descendant of the Tzemach Tzedek and the great-grandfather of the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe. He maintained the profound spiritual legacy of the Schneerson lineage during the late nineteenth century.

Rabbi Yaakov Yisrael of Cherkasy was a prominent leader of the Chernobyl dynasty and the son-in-law of the Mitteler Rebbe. His marriage formed a historic alliance between the Chabad and Chernobyl Hasidic dynasties, and he established a massive following in Ukraine.

Rebbetzin Menucha Rochel Slonim was the illustrious daughter of the Mitteler Rebbe. Making Aliyah to the Holy Land in 1845, she became the undisputed matriarch and leader of the Chabad community in Hebron, revered by Jews and Arabs alike for her blessings and wisdom.
Rebbetzin Sara was a daughter of the Mitteler Rebbe. She grew up in the spiritual epicenter of the Chabad movement, though she tragically passed away in her youth.

Rebbetzin Devorah Leah was a daughter of the Mitteler Rebbe who married Rabbi Yaakov Yisrael of Cherkasy. Her marriage solidified a powerful and historic familial alliance between the Chabad and Chernobyl Hasidic dynasties.

Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka was the daughter of the Rebbe Maharash and the sister of the Rebbe Rashab. She was deeply respected within the inner circles of the Chabad court during the movement's turbulent transition periods.

Rabbi Shmaryahu Schneerson was an uncle to the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe and the brother of Rabbi Levi Yitzchak. He was a dedicated scholar who navigated the immense challenges of maintaining Jewish life under oppressive Russian regimes.

Rabbi Dovber Schneerson was the brother of the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe. Trapped in the Soviet Union during World War II, he suffered terribly and was tragically murdered during the Holocaust, deeply mourned by his family.

Rabbi Yitzchak Schneersohn was a descendant of the Chabad Rebbes who became highly active in public and communal life. Operating primarily in Kishinev and later in Paris, he played a notable role in Jewish administration and historical preservation.
